title: Accelerating point defect simulations using data-driven and machine learning approaches
authors: Arun Mannodi-Kanakkithodi, Menglin Huang, Prashun Gorai, Seán R. Kavanagh
abstract: Point defects in solid-state materials are now routinely simulated using large supercell structures, requiring efficient quantum mechanical solutions. Data-driven and machine learning (ML) models trained on computational data can enable rapid defect property predictions and high-throughput screening. In this article, we provide an overview of prominent efforts to accelerate defect simulations using these approaches. We begin by discussing the motivations for data-driven techniques in defect modeling, and describe efforts over the past decade to use descriptor-based models for rapid screening of defect properties -- most notably in oxides. In particular, we discuss case studies where surrogate models and interatomic potentials were trained on density functional theory (DFT) data, leading to predictions with quantum-mechanical accuracies at a fraction of the cost. In addition to geometry relaxation and formation energy predictions, these interatomic potentials are capable of predicting phonon modes and vibrational free energies to yield defect energetics at finite temperatures -- representing a key frontier for computational defect research. We finish with a discussion on how to connect these approaches and their outputs with experimental data, and provide an outlook on this burgeoning sub-field.
summary: This review article examines the transition from expensive first-principles simulations to efficient machine learning-driven approaches for studying point defects in solids. It covers the evolution from simple empirical descriptors to sophisticated equivariant graph neural networks and interatomic potentials. The authors highlight how these methods enable high-throughput screening of defect properties like formation energies and migration pathways. Ultimately, the paper discusses the integration of these computational tools with experimental data to accelerate materials discovery.
main_problem: The high computational cost of using Density Functional Theory (DFT) to simulate point defects in large supercells limits the ability to perform high-throughput screening and study complex defect properties.
main_result: The paper reviews how machine learning approaches, including descriptor-based models and machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs), can achieve quantum-mechanical accuracy at a fraction of the cost, enabling the prediction of formation energies, charge transition levels, and vibrational properties.
method: A review of data-driven techniques including descriptor-based regression, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), and Machine-Learned Force Fields (MLFFs) trained on DFT data (GGA, HSE, GW).
model_or_system: Point defects (vacancies, interstitials, and dopants) in solid-state materials such as metal oxides, semiconductors (GaN, SiC, CdTe), and halide perovskites.
key_observables: Defect formation energies, charge transition levels, migration barriers, phonon modes, and vibrational free energies.
important_parameters: Chemical potentials, Fermi levels, band gaps, and electronegativity differences.
main_assumptions: Many models assume the dilute limit (low defect concentration) and often rely on semi-local DFT data which may lack accuracy for certain electronic properties.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates DFT considerations like symmetry-breaking and level-of-theory comparisons; Figure 2 shows descriptor-based energy predictions and importance rankings; Figure 3 compares potential energy surfaces between DFT and MLFFs.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the motivation for accelerated defect modeling, progresses through descriptor-based models and interatomic potentials, discusses advanced applications like vibrational properties, and concludes with the connection to experimental data and future outlook.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(b) The level of theory (e.g., semi-local GGA vs non-local hybrid DFT) determines accuracy of the band gap and band edges, which could also be physically shifted to match experimental values.25, 26 VB = valence band, CB = conduction band, Eg = band gap, and the dashed lines are hypothetical defect levels. Also pictured is a comparison between defect formation energy diagrams in GaN from GGA and HSE, reproduced with permission from Lyons et al.27 (c) Intentional symmetry-breaking in defect coordination environments...
